standard_description: Describe characters in a story (e.g., their traits, motivations, or feelings) and explain how their actions contribute to the sequence of events.
subject: Reading

khan_exercises.title: Extreme Environments: reading realistic fiction; The Adventures of Fisher
khan_exercises.description: 3rd grade reading comprehension practice using realistic fiction
topic_path: Extreme Environments: reading realistic fiction; The Adventures of Fisher

items.question_content:
Read the two passages, then answer the practice question.

___

##Fisher on Top of the World##


1. Before Dad and I traveled to the [[☃ definition 1]] mountains in Peru, I barely ever thought about llamas, besides the ones printed on my favorite socks. That all changed after our time in the *tierra helada*, meaning “frozen place”.
 
2. Bundled in the heaviest gear, we arrived in the village we’d be staying in. A severe storm had just blown through. People were scattered around, calling out down the slope in frightened tones. Dad and his translator went to investigate.
 
3. “Fisher, come out and help. A llama got separated from the pack in the blizzard!” Dad said when they returned.
 
4. Uncertain, I followed anyway. *It must be so hard to live here*, I thought. The ground is so steep and slick; it’s amazing everyone didn’t fall off the mountain. This was the coldest place I’d ever been to by far and I didn’t ever think I’d get warm again. We’d been traveling for months, so Dad could do research for his book, *Living To the Extreme*. At that moment, I was ready to go back to our very temperate climate in California. 
 
5. I ran to Dad’s side nonetheless. He was scanning the area with a set of binoculars.
 
6. “Dad, I feel bad about the llama. But this kind of thing must happen all the time. It’s weird that people stay in such a harsh place,” I said.
 
7. “People are more adaptable than you think," said Dad. "They build their cultures around where and how they live.” My dad's an anthropologist—that means he studies humans, and how our societies, cultures, and languages are similar and different around the world.
 
8. His words made me think about our mountain journey. People *did* do a lot to survive. They grew certain crops according to where they lived. Then they bartered and sold them up and down the mountain—it’s called the vertical trade. Llamas were at the heart of it. Loaded down with bags of potatoes, coffee, or bananas, they were designed for the steep treks.
 
9. As the wind shifted, I heard a soft bleating grow louder. I peered down to a cluster of rocks as a llama limped into view. I frantically pointed, shouting, “Over here!”

 ![Fisher pointing to a llama that is stuck in the snow.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/534f9a3668049fcb8e3f5d447b1aa696.jpg)

10. Several villagers raced to the llama and carried it up as everyone cheered. Already, I was starting to warm up.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

___

##Fisher’s Trip Down Under##
 
1. I tossed my knapsack onto the hotel room floor and flopped onto the bed. 

2. “Ugh, jetlag,” I groaned. “Can we skip the tour?” 

3. For the past year, Dad and I had been bouncing around the world so he could research his book about living in extreme climates. Our latest stop was Coober Pedy, an Australian mining town and one of the hottest places in the world. Lately, I've felt kind of “meh” about traveling.

4. “You know why we’re here, Fisher—to learn about how people live in their unique environments. You can’t do that from a hotel room,” Dad said. 

5. I pointed to the jagged stone ceiling.

6. “Well, I just did. Right on the hotel website, it says the hotel, like most buildings here, is built underground because of the intense desert heat,” I said.
 
7. “Okay, smarty, that’s a start,” Dad chuckled. “But facts are only a gateway to the real story. You have to get out to find the real flavor of a place. We could have fun too.” 
 
8. I didn’t want to upset Dad so I went anyway. That afternoon, we toured a kangaroo orphanage and then went inside the dugout where Ms. Hastings, the town historian, lived. She showed us a picture of the [[☃ definition 2]] opal gemstone the area is famous for.  I couldn't take my eyes off it.

9. “That’s amazing,” I said.

10. She laughed. “You’re not the only one to think that. Miners from more than 50 countries have come to find their fortunes here over the years. Many stayed for good. Any way they could, they made this place home,” she said. “Today, Coober has Chinese and pizza restaurants, olive groves from Greece, and a drive-in movie theater. You’ll have a great time here.”

11. “Thanks so much for the tour—but I think Fisher wants to get back to the room for a bit,” Dad said.

12. Dad was wrong though. I had gooey cheese, glittering gems, and more on my mind. 

13. “Dad, let’s—”

14. “Keep going?”

15. “Ok, ok, you were right,” I said, with a laugh. “This is much better than staying inside. Let’s get pizza.”

16. As we set off, Ms. Hastings called, “Make sure to try the emu sausage.” 

17. We made sure we did. And for the next few days, I “tasted” as many flavors of Coober Pedy as I possibly could.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

___

##Practice Question##

**Pick THREE character traits that best describe Fisher.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: a long mountain range that is located along the western side of South America
shiny and reflective, rainbow-colored

4. Uncertain, I followed anyway. *It must be so hard to live here*, I thought. The ground is so steep and slick; it’s amazing everyone didn’t fall off the mountain. This was the coldest place I’d ever been to by far and I didn’t ever think I’d get warm again. We’d been traveling for months, so Dad could do research for his book, *Living To the Extreme*. At that moment, I was ready to go back to our very temperate climate in California. 
 
5. I ran to Dad’s side nonetheless. He was scanning the area with a set of binoculars.
 
6. “Dad, I feel bad about the llama. But this kind of thing must happen all the time. It’s weird that people stay in such a harsh place,” I said.
 
7. “People are more adaptable than you think," said Dad. "They build their cultures around where and how they live.” My dad's an anthropologist—that means he studies humans, and how our societies, cultures, and languages are similar and different around the world.
 
8. His words made me think about our mountain journey. People *did* do a lot to survive. They grew certain crops according to where they lived. Then they bartered and sold them up and down the mountain—it’s called the vertical trade. Llamas were at the heart of it. Loaded down with bags of potatoes, coffee, or bananas, they were designed for the steep treks.
 
9. As the wind shifted, I heard a soft bleating grow louder. I peered down to a cluster of rocks as a llama limped into view. I frantically pointed, shouting, “Over here!”

 ![Fisher pointing to a llama that is stuck in the snow.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/534f9a3668049fcb8e3f5d447b1aa696.jpg)
 
10. Several villagers raced to the llama and carried it up as everyone cheered. Already, I was starting to warm up.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

___

##Fisher’s Trip Down Under##
 
1. I tossed my knapsack onto the hotel room floor and flopped onto the bed. 

2. “Ugh, jetlag,” I groaned. “Can we skip the tour?” 

3. For the past year, Dad and I had been bouncing around the world so he could research his book about living in extreme climates. Our latest stop was Coober Pedy, an Australian mining town and one of the hottest places in the world. Lately, I've felt kind of “meh” about traveling.

4. “You know why we’re here, Fisher—to learn about how people live in their unique environments. You can’t do that from a hotel room,” Dad said. 

5. I pointed to the jagged stone ceiling.

6. “Well, I just did. Right on the hotel website, it says the hotel, like most buildings here, is built underground because of the intense desert heat,” I said.
 
7. “Okay, smarty, that’s a start,” Dad chuckled. “But facts are only a gateway to the real story. You have to get out to find the real flavor of a place. We could have fun too.” 
 
8. I didn’t want to upset Dad so I went anyway. That afternoon, we toured a kangaroo orphanage and then went inside the dugout where Ms. Hastings, the town historian, lived. She showed us a picture of the [[☃ definition 2]] opal gemstone the area is famous for.  I couldn't take my eyes off it.

9. “That’s amazing,” I said.

10. She laughed. “You’re not the only one to think that. Miners from more than 50 countries have come to find their fortunes here over the years. Many stayed for good. Any way they could, they made this place home,” she said. “Today, Coober has Chinese and pizza restaurants, olive groves from Greece, and a drive-in movie theater. You’ll have a great time here.”

11. “Thanks so much for the tour—but I think Fisher wants to get back to the room for a bit,” Dad said.

12. Dad was wrong though. I had gooey cheese, glittering gems, and more on my mind. 

13. “Dad, let’s—”

14. “Keep going?”

15. “Ok, ok, you were right,” I said, with a laugh. “This is much better than staying inside. Let’s get pizza.”

16. As we set off, Ms. Hastings called, “Make sure to try the emu sausage.” 

17. We made sure we did. And for the next few days, I “tasted” as many flavors of Coober Pedy as I possibly could.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

___

##Practice Question##

**Pick TWO details that describe the different settings of “Fisher On Top of the World” and “Fisher’s Trip Down Under.”**

##A New Pet##

![Diego is lying back on a crescent moon in the night sky, asleep. A thought bubble shows him dreaming of a grey dog with floppy ears.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c4fcaac6bde993440a04120f32af87c7.png)
*Picture 1*

1. For as long as Diego could remember he wanted a dog. Hardly a week would go by without him pestering his grandmother about it. And every time, her answer was the same. “No, Diego we can’t get a dog! We live in a small two-bedroom apartment in the city. We’re already bursting at the seams. Not to mention taking a dog for a walk is difficult. We have the disadvantage of living a full six blocks from the nearest park. How is a dog supposed to get exercise every day? Plus, we would have to find time to groom a dog.”
 
2. Diego’s response was always the same. “I promise to walk the dog every day, Abuela. You wouldn’t have to do anything!”
 
3. But Diego’s grandmother never gave in. One day after school, Diego’s grandmother told Diego they were going out. “Where are we going, Abuela?” Diego asked. 
 
4. “Just come with me,” replied his grandmother.
 
5. *Where on earth are we going?* Diego wondered as they walked. *It’s a school night.*

![Diego and his grandmother walking outside. Diego looks happy and curious; his grandmother looks a little serious.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d49b089ec5e68a8719df01ff111dcfab.png)
 *Picture 2*

6. To Diego’s shock, his grandmother led him to the animal shelter. As they entered, Diego’s grandmother turned around and said, “Diego, you’ve worked so hard at school and I am very proud. To show you how responsible I think you are, I am going to let you get a pet. But it can’t be a dog. We simply don’t have the room for a dog. And we don’t have a yard for a dog to play in. You can pick either a fish or a hamster.”
 
7. Diego felt disappointed. A fish or a hamster was nothing like a dog! Fish and hamsters weren’t loyal and sweet. They didn’t show affection to their owners. Diego didn’t think either of them would be a good companion. *But maybe if I show Abuela how responsible I am with a pet, she’ll eventually let me get a dog,* he thought. 
 
8. Diego considered both the fish and the hamster. When he took the hamster from the cage, he realized that, in fact, a hamster was similar to a dog after all. The hamster showed Diego affection by nuzzling his hand. The hamster’s soft hair could be groomed. But more importantly, that little hamster provided comfort to Diego and would be a nice companion. “I’ll call you Phil,” Diego declared to his new friend, as he gently petted Phil’s soft spotted fur. 

![Diego holding Phil the hamster. Diego looks very happy.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2b7d13833c0abe9c17076190d70f9365.png)
*Picture 3* 

[[☃ explanation 1]]

khan_exercises.title: Homes: reading realistic fiction; A New Home
khan_exercises.description: 3rd grade reading comprehension practice using realistic fiction
topic_path: Homes: reading realistic fiction; A New Home

items.question_content:
Read the story, then answer the TWO practice questions. 

___

##A New Home##

Luciana pinched her eyes closed for what felt like the hundredth time. She just couldn’t sleep—she was excited, yes. But she was worried, too. 

![Luciana lying in her dark bedroom at night, wide awake.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bb7d0397417c6283ab636cc67889ca45.jpg)
*Picture 1*

“I don’t have to feel worried,” she whispered into the dark. “Tomorrow we will all be together again. Papi says that California is a lot like Mexico. A new country and a new home isn’t bad. New beginnings can be good.”

Luciana turned onto her side and let out a slow, calming breath. She reached out and pressed her palm against the cool, smooth adobe stone wall next to her. “My new house will have walls just like these,” she whispered. “They will be tall and strong and will keep me safe.” Luciana inhaled again and pushed out another slow, cool breath.

*And Papi will be there*, she thought, a familiar smile tugging at her lips. Luciana hadn’t seen Papi for nearly a year, since the time he first left to work on the farms in America.  She’d been waiting for so long for tomorrow to come—the day they’d all be together again! 

Luciana pulled her arm back from the wall, grasping the blankets around her and tucking them beneath her chin. *Papi will teach me everything I need to know about our new home*. Luciana took one last deep pull of air and pushed it out slowly, inviting sleep.

Luciana gripped Mami’s hand tightly as they stepped off the bus. She turned and watched, wanting to make sure that Abuela stepped off after her. She grabbed Abuela’s hand before turning to squint into the crowd standing under the blazing sunlight.

“Luciana!”

Luciana whipped around to see a man rushing forward. 
“Papi!” Luciana called out. She dropped Mami’s and Abuela’s hands, running to meet Papi’s outstretched arms.
“I’m so glad you’re here,” Papi whispered as he hugged her tightly. “Let’s go see your new home.”

Luciana smiled. Her new home was made of red stone, just like her old one.

She stepped closer, eager to press her palm to the smooth, cooling stone. But something was wrong. This stone wasn’t cool or smooth. It felt rough, grabbing and clawing at her skin. She pulled her hand back and peered closely at the perfect rectangular blocks.

“What’s this stone?” she turned and asked Papi.

“It’s brick,” he replied. “Brick houses are very strong and stable, Luci. I want the strongest for my best girl.”

Luciana nodded.

![Luciana feeling the brick wall of her new home, while her Papi smiles at her.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/29616df450fa676f82d1437d9debe52b.jpg)
*Picture 2*

“Good,” she said. “It is different, but it’s good. I like it, Papi.”

Papi’s face broke into a wide smile as he opened the front door and led the family inside.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

___

##Practice questions##

This question has two parts. Answer Part A, then Part B.

###Part A###

**What is the central idea of the story?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

###Part B###

**Which detail best supports the answer to Part A?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

*Psst! Don't forget to choose an answer for both questions* :)

options.explanation: Estimated 610-800L

choices.content: Change can be scary, but it can also be positive.
The best houses are made of stone.
It is best not to worry about things.
Things should always stay the same, no matter what.
“New beginnings can be good.”
“My new house will have walls just like these,”
“I don’t have to feel worried.”
“They will be tall and strong and will keep me safe.”

##A New Home##

Luciana pinched her eyes closed for what felt like the hundredth time. She just couldn’t sleep—she was excited, yes. But she was worried, too. 

![Luciana lying in her dark bedroom at night, wide awake.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bb7d0397417c6283ab636cc67889ca45.jpg)
*Picture 1*

“I don’t have to feel worried,” she whispered into the dark. “Tomorrow we will all be together again. Papi says that California is a lot like Mexico. A new country and a new home isn’t bad. New beginnings can be good.”

Luciana turned onto her side and let out a slow, calming breath. She reached out and pressed her palm against the cool, smooth adobe stone wall next to her. “My new house will have walls just like these,” she whispered. “They will be tall and strong and will keep me safe.” Luciana inhaled again and pushed out another slow, cool breath.

*And Papi will be there*, she thought, a familiar smile tugging at her lips. Luciana hadn’t seen Papi for nearly a year, since the time he first left to work on the farms in America.  She’d been waiting for so long for tomorrow to come—the day they’d all be together again! 

Luciana pulled her arm back from the wall, grasping the blankets around her and tucking them beneath her chin. *Papi will teach me everything I need to know about our new home*. Luciana took one last deep pull of air and pushed it out slowly, inviting sleep.

Luciana gripped Mami’s hand tightly as they stepped off the bus. She turned and watched, wanting to make sure that Abuela stepped off after her. She grabbed Abuela’s hand before turning to squint into the crowd standing under the blazing sunlight.

“Luciana!”

Luciana whipped around to see a man rushing forward. 
“Papi!” Luciana called out. She dropped Mami’s and Abuela’s hands, running to meet Papi’s outstretched arms.
“I’m so glad you’re here,” Papi whispered as he hugged her tightly. “Let’s go see your new home.”

Luciana smiled. Her new home was made of red stone, just like her old one.

She stepped closer, eager to press her palm to the smooth, cooling stone. But something was wrong. This stone wasn’t cool or smooth. It felt rough, grabbing and clawing at her skin. She pulled her hand back and peered closely at the perfect rectangular blocks.

“What’s this stone?” she turned and asked Papi.

“It’s brick,” he replied. “Brick houses are very strong and stable, Luci. I want the strongest for my best girl.”

Luciana nodded.

![Luciana feeling the brick wall of her new home, while her Papi smiles at her.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/29616df450fa676f82d1437d9debe52b.jpg)
*Picture 2*

“Good,” she said. “It is different, but it’s good. I like it, Papi.”

Papi’s face broke into a wide smile as he opened the front door and led the family inside.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

___

“‘I want the strongest for my best girl.’”
“‘They will be tall and strong and will keep me safe.’”

“It’s brick,” he replied. “Brick houses are very strong and stable, Luci. I want the strongest for my best girl.”

Luciana nodded.

![Luciana feeling the brick wall of her new home, while her Papi smiles at her.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/29616df450fa676f82d1437d9debe52b.jpg)
*Picture 2* 

“Good,” she said. “It is different, but it’s good. I like it, Papi.”

Papi’s face broke into a wide smile as he opened the front door and led the family inside.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

khan_exercises.title: Homes: reading realistic fiction; Feels Like Home
khan_exercises.description: 3rd grade reading comprehension practice using realistic fiction
topic_path: Homes: reading realistic fiction; Feels Like Home

items.question_content:
Read the passage, then answer the TWO practice questions. 

___

##Feels Like Home##

1. Luciana stared through the glass, the blur of trees and sky passing by the window, marking the spaces between the bus stops. She yawned, her heavy eyelids drooping. She’d been in her new home two weeks now, but she had a hard time sleeping. She missed her old home. 

2. “I know what you need,” Abuela had said when Luciana had confided in her a few days ago. “Come with me.”

3. Abuela had walked into the kitchen and pulled a cookie sheet from the cabinet. Luciana thought maybe Abuela was going to cook one of her favorite meals to comfort her. But Abuela had opened the kitchen door and walked outside, carrying the cookie sheet in her hand.

4. Luciana had followed her into the yard. Abuela had handed her a small shovel and picked up a trowel herself. “Dig here,” she’d said. Luciana was confused, but did as she’d been told. “Abuela?” she’d begun, but Abuela had shushed her and pointed at the small pile of dirt. Luciana dug a bit more until the sandy soil gave way to cool, red clay.

5. Abuela wet the pile of earth with a nearby water hose before mixing the clay and sandy soil with her hands.

6. “Help me mix,” she’d said, taking Luciana’s hands and pushing them into the mixture. She’d laughed at Luciana’s face, her nose scrunched up, her brows furrowed in confusion.

7. “Now here,” she’d said, pointing to the cookie sheet. She’d watched as Luciana placed handfuls of the mud onto the tray, then had run her hand along the top of the sheet to smooth it before using her trowel to cut lines through the wet mud.
8. “Abuela, I don’t understand,” Luciana had said. “How is mud on a tray going to help me?”

9. “Be patient,” Abuela had replied. “You will see.”

10. The bus lurched to a stop, bringing Luciana’s mind back to the present. She stood and made her way off the bus and down the narrow path to her home.

11.  She smiled when she spotted Abuela waiting for her.

12. “I have a surprise for you,” Abuela said as Luciana approached. “Come with me.”

13. Luciana followed Abuela onto the porch, where she spotted the metal tray. Only the mud wasn’t mud anymore. A few days’ baking in the sunlight had hardened it to stone.

14. “Is it . . .” Luciana’s voice trailed off as she stepped closer to the tray. She pressed her hand against the smooth surface. Tears brimmed in her eyes as she turned and looked at Abuela. “It’s adobe, isn’t it?” she asked. “Just like the walls of our old house in Mexico.” 

15. Abuela smiled and nodded. “I miss our old home, too,” she whispered. “But home is always with us, Luci. You, me, Mami, Papi . . . we are what make a home.”

16. That night, Luciana pulled the covers up under her chin and closed her eyes. She reached out and placed her hand on the cool, smooth piece of stone she’d placed on her nightstand, and smiled.  

![Luciana, while tucked into bed, reaches out to touch the adobe on her nightstand.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5e88f444fdee1b480d12dcdfd21b3cb6.jpg)

[[☃ explanation 1]]

___

##Practice questions##

This question has two parts. Answer Part A, then Part B.

###Part A###

**How does the ending of the story change Luciana’s feelings about her new home?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

###Part B###

**What detail from the passage best supports the answer to Part A?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

*Psst! Don't forget to choose an answer for both questions* :)

options.explanation:
Estimated 610-800L

*Text source: [“Appropriate Mixes for Adobe”](http://www.greenhomebuilding.com/QandA/adobe/mixes.htm) by [Green Home Building](http://www.greenhomebuilding.com/index.htm)*

choices.content: She realizes that home is not the building itself, but the people who share it.
She decides she likes the way the brick on her new house feels.
She hopes that one day she can go back and see her old house again.
She wonders if she can replace the brick on her house with adobe.
“‘You, me, Mami, Papi . . . we are what make a  home.’”
“‘I miss our old home, too,’ she whispered.”
“‘Just like the walls of our old house in Mexico.’”
“She pressed her hand against the smooth surface.”

7. “Now here,” she’d said, pointing to the cookie sheet. She’d watched as Luciana placed handfuls of the mud onto the tray, then had run her hand along the top of the sheet to smooth it before using her trowel to cut lines through the wet mud.
8. “Abuela, I don’t understand,” Luciana had said. “How is mud on a tray going to help me?”

9. “Be patient,” Abuela had replied. “You will see.”

10. The bus lurched to a stop, bringing Luciana’s mind back to the present. She stood and made her way off the bus and down the narrow path to her home.

11.  She smiled when she spotted Abuela waiting for her.

12. “I have a surprise for you,” Abuela said as Luciana approached. “Come with me.”

13. Luciana followed Abuela onto the porch, where she spotted the metal tray. Only the mud wasn’t mud anymore. A few days’ baking in the sunlight had hardened it to stone.

14. “Is it . . .” Luciana’s voice trailed off as she stepped closer to the tray. She pressed her hand against the smooth surface. Tears brimmed in her eyes as she turned and looked at Abuela. “It’s adobe, isn’t it?” she asked. “Just like the walls of our old house in Mexico.” 

15. Abuela smiled and nodded. “I miss our old home, too,” she whispered. “But home is always with us, Luci. You, me, Mami, Papi . . . we are what make a home.”

16. That night, Luciana pulled the covers up under her chin and closed her eyes. She reached out and placed her hand on the cool, smooth piece of stone she’d placed on her nightstand, and smiled.  

![Luciana, while tucked into bed, reaches out to touch the adobe on her nightstand.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5e88f444fdee1b480d12dcdfd21b3cb6.jpg)

[[☃ explanation 1]]

___

##Feels Like Home##

1. Luciana stared through the glass, the blur of trees and sky passing by the window, marking the spaces between the bus stops. She yawned, her heavy eyelids drooping. She’d been in her new home two weeks now, but she had a hard time sleeping. She missed her old home. 

2. “I know what you need,” Abuela had said when Luciana had confided in her a few days ago. “Come with me.”

3. Abuela had walked into the kitchen and pulled a cookie sheet from the cabinet. Luciana thought maybe Abuela was going to cook one of her favorite meals to comfort her. But Abuela had opened the kitchen door and walked outside, carrying the cookie sheet in her hand.

4. Luciana had followed her into the yard. Abuela had handed her a small shovel and picked up a trowel herself. “Dig here,” she’d said. Luciana was confused, but did as she’d been told. “Abuela?” she’d begun, but Abuela had shushed her and pointed at the small pile of dirt. Luciana dug a bit more until the sandy soil gave way to cool, red clay.

5. Abuela wet the pile of earth with a nearby water hose before mixing the clay and sandy soil with her hands.

6. “Help me mix,” she’d said, taking Luciana’s hands and pushing them into the mixture. She’d laughed at Luciana’s face, her nose scrunched up, her brows furrowed in confusion.

7. “Now here,” she’d said, pointing to the cookie sheet. She’d watched as Luciana placed handfuls of the mud onto the tray, then had run her hand along the top of the sheet to smooth it before using her trowel to cut lines through the wet mud.

9. “Be patient,” Abuela had replied. “You will see.”

11.  She smiled when she spotted Abuela waiting for her.

##A New Home##

Luciana pinched her eyes closed for what felt like the hundredth time. She just couldn’t sleep—she was excited, yes. But she was worried, too. 

![Luciana lying in her dark bedroom at night, wide awake.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bb7d0397417c6283ab636cc67889ca45.jpg)
*Picture 1*

“I don’t have to feel worried,” she whispered into the dark. “Tomorrow we will all be together again. Papi says that California is a lot like Mexico. A new country and a new home isn’t bad. New beginnings can be good.”

Luciana turned onto her side and let out a slow, calming breath. She reached out and pressed her palm against the cool, smooth adobe stone wall next to her. “My new house will have walls just like these,” she whispered. “They will be tall and strong and will keep me safe.” Luciana inhaled again and pushed out another slow, cool breath.

*And Papi will be there*, she thought, a familiar smile tugging at her lips. Luciana hadn’t seen Papi for nearly a year, since the time he first left to work on the farms in America.  She’d been waiting for so long for tomorrow to come—the day they’d all be together again! 

Luciana pulled her arm back from the wall, grasping the blankets around her and tucking them beneath her chin. *Papi will teach me everything I need to know about our new home*. Luciana took one last deep pull of air and pushed it out slowly, inviting sleep.

Luciana gripped Mami’s hand tightly as they stepped off the bus. She turned and watched, wanting to make sure that Abuela stepped off after her. She grabbed Abuela’s hand before turning to squint into the crowd standing under the blazing sunlight.

“Luciana!”

Luciana whipped around to see a man rushing forward. 
“Papi!” Luciana called out. She dropped Mami’s and Abuela’s hands, running to meet Papi’s outstretched arms.
“I’m so glad you’re here,” Papi whispered as he hugged her tightly. “Let’s go see your new home.”

Luciana smiled. Her new home was made of red stone, just like her old one.

She stepped closer, eager to press her palm to the smooth, cooling stone. But something was wrong. This stone wasn’t cool or smooth. It felt rough, grabbing and clawing at her skin. She pulled her hand back and peered closely at the perfect rectangular blocks.

“What’s this stone?” she turned and asked Papi.

“It’s brick,” he replied. “Brick houses are very strong and stable, Luci. I want the strongest for my best girl.”

Luciana nodded.

![Luciana feeling the brick wall of her new home, while her Papi smiles at her.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/29616df450fa676f82d1437d9debe52b.jpg)
*Picture 2*

“Good,” she said. “It is different, but it’s good. I like it, Papi.”

Papi’s face broke into a wide smile as he opened the front door and led the family inside.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

___

6. “Help me mix,” she’d said, taking Luciana’s hands and pushing them into the mixture. She’d laughed at Luciana’s face, her nose scrunched up, her brows furrowed in confusion.

7. “Now here,” she’d said, pointing to the cookie sheet. She’d watched as Luciana placed handfuls of the mud onto the tray, then had run her hand along the top of the sheet to smooth it before using her trowel to cut lines through the wet mud.
8. “Abuela, I don’t understand,” Luciana had said. “How is mud on a tray going to help me?”

9. “Be patient,” Abuela had replied. “You will see.”

10. The bus lurched to a stop, bringing Luciana’s mind back to the present. She stood and made her way off the bus and down the narrow path to her home.

11.  She smiled when she spotted Abuela waiting for her.

12. “I have a surprise for you,” Abuela said as Luciana approached. “Come with me.”

13. Luciana followed Abuela onto the porch, where she spotted the metal tray. Only the mud wasn’t mud anymore. A few days’ baking in the sunlight had hardened it to stone.

14. “Is it . . .” Luciana’s voice trailed off as she stepped closer to the tray. She pressed her hand against the smooth surface. Tears brimmed in her eyes as she turned and looked at Abuela. “It’s adobe, isn’t it?” she asked. “Just like the walls of our old house in Mexico.” 

15. Abuela smiled and nodded. “I miss our old home, too,” she whispered. “But home is always with us, Luci. You, me, Mami, Papi . . . we are what make a home.”

16. That night, Luciana pulled the covers up under her chin and closed her eyes. She reached out and placed her hand on the cool, smooth piece of stone she’d placed on her nightstand, and smiled.  

![Luciana, while tucked into bed, reaches out to touch the adobe on her nightstand.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5e88f444fdee1b480d12dcdfd21b3cb6.jpg)

[[☃ explanation 1]]

**Select whether each detail is from “A New Home”, “Feels Like Home”, or both.**

Luciana rides a bus.

##A New Home##

Luciana pinched her eyes closed for what felt like the hundredth time. She just couldn’t sleep—she was excited, yes. But she was worried, too. 

![Luciana lying in her dark bedroom at night, wide awake.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bb7d0397417c6283ab636cc67889ca45.jpg)
*Picture 1*

“I don’t have to feel worried,” she whispered into the dark. “Tomorrow we will all be together again. Papi says that California is a lot like Mexico. A new country and a new home isn’t bad. New beginnings can be good.”

Luciana turned onto her side and let out a slow, calming breath. She reached out and pressed her palm against the cool, smooth adobe stone wall next to her. “My new house will have walls just like these,” she whispered. “They will be tall and strong and will keep me safe.” Luciana inhaled again and pushed out another slow, cool breath.

*And Papi will be there*, she thought, a familiar smile tugging at her lips. Luciana hadn’t seen Papi for nearly a year, since the time he first left to work on the farms in America.  She’d been waiting for so long for tomorrow to come—the day they’d all be together again! 

Luciana pulled her arm back from the wall, grasping the blankets around her and tucking them beneath her chin. *Papi will teach me everything I need to know about our new home*. Luciana took one last deep pull of air and pushed it out slowly, inviting sleep.

Luciana gripped Mami’s hand tightly as they stepped off the bus. She turned and watched, wanting to make sure that Abuela stepped off after her. She grabbed Abuela’s hand before turning to squint into the crowd standing under the blazing sunlight.

“Luciana!”

Luciana whipped around to see a man rushing forward. 
“Papi!” Luciana called out. She dropped Mami’s and Abuela’s hands, running to meet Papi’s outstretched arms.
“I’m so glad you’re here,” Papi whispered as he hugged her tightly. “Let’s go see your new home.”

Luciana smiled. Her new home was made of red stone, just like her old one.

She stepped closer, eager to press her palm to the smooth, cooling stone. But something was wrong. This stone wasn’t cool or smooth. It felt rough, grabbing and clawing at her skin. She pulled her hand back and peered closely at the perfect rectangular blocks.

“What’s this stone?” she turned and asked Papi.

“It’s brick,” he replied. “Brick houses are very strong and stable, Luci. I want the strongest for my best girl.”

Luciana nodded.

![Luciana feeling the brick wall of her new home, while her Papi smiles at her.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/29616df450fa676f82d1437d9debe52b.jpg)
*Picture 2*

“Good,” she said. “It is different, but it’s good. I like it, Papi.”

Papi’s face broke into a wide smile as he opened the front door and led the family inside.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

___

2. “I know what you need,” Abuela had said when Luciana had confided in her a few days ago. “Come with me.”

3. Abuela had walked into the kitchen and pulled a cookie sheet from the cabinet. Luciana thought maybe Abuela was going to cook one of her favorite meals to comfort her. But Abuela had opened the kitchen door and walked outside, carrying the cookie sheet in her hand.

4. Luciana had followed her into the yard. Abuela had handed her a small shovel and picked up a trowel herself. “Dig here,” she’d said. Luciana was confused, but did as she’d been told. “Abuela?” she’d begun, but Abuela had shushed her and pointed at the small pile of dirt. Luciana dug a bit more until the sandy soil gave way to cool, red clay.

5. Abuela wet the pile of earth with a nearby water hose before mixing the clay and sandy soil with her hands.

6. “Help me mix,” she’d said, taking Luciana’s hands and pushing them into the mixture. She’d laughed at Luciana’s face, her nose scrunched up, her brows furrowed in confusion.

7. “Now here,” she’d said, pointing to the cookie sheet. She’d watched as Luciana placed handfuls of the mud onto the tray, then had run her hand along the top of the sheet to smooth it before using her trowel to cut lines through the wet mud.
8. “Abuela, I don’t understand,” Luciana had said. “How is mud on a tray going to help me?”

9. “Be patient,” Abuela had replied. “You will see.”

10. The bus lurched to a stop, bringing Luciana’s mind back to the present. She stood and made her way off the bus and down the narrow path to her home.

11.  She smiled when she spotted Abuela waiting for her.

12. “I have a surprise for you,” Abuela said as Luciana approached. “Come with me.”

13. Luciana followed Abuela onto the porch, where she spotted the metal tray. Only the mud wasn’t mud anymore. A few days’ baking in the sunlight had hardened it to stone.

14. “Is it . . .” Luciana’s voice trailed off as she stepped closer to the tray. She pressed her hand against the smooth surface. Tears brimmed in her eyes as she turned and looked at Abuela. “It’s adobe, isn’t it?” she asked. “Just like the walls of our old house in Mexico.” 

15. Abuela smiled and nodded. “I miss our old home, too,” she whispered. “But home is always with us, Luci. You, me, Mami, Papi . . . we are what make a home.”

16. That night, Luciana pulled the covers up under her chin and closed her eyes. She reached out and placed her hand on the cool, smooth piece of stone she’d placed on her nightstand, and smiled.  

![Luciana, while tucked into bed, reaches out to touch the adobe on her nightstand.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5e88f444fdee1b480d12dcdfd21b3cb6.jpg)

[[☃ explanation 1]]

___

##Practice question##

**How are the problems of the two stories different?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: In “A New Home”, Luciana feels nervous about moving. In “Feels Like Home”,  Luciana feels homesick for her house in Mexico.
In “A New Home”, Luciana doesn’t want to ride on a bus. In “Feels Like Home”, Luciana has trouble sleeping.
In “A New Home”, Luciana feels angry that she has to move. In “Feels Like Home”, Luciana feels like her home is too crowded.
In “A New Home”, Luciana isn’t ready to help make adobe stones. In “Feels Like Home”, she doesn’t want to listen to Abuela.

choices.content: “A New Home” teaches us that change can be scary but it can also be good.  “Feels Like Home” teaches us that people, not buildings,  are what makes us feel at home.
“A New Home” teaches us that homes can be made of different stones. “Feels Like Home” shows that brick is a good stone for a home.
“A New Home” shows that families shouldn’t be separated for a very long time. “Feels Like Home” shows that grandparents are important parts of the family.
“A New Home” teaches that feelings can’t be changed. “Feels Like Home” shows that it’s important to do crafts with your family.

khan_exercises.description: 3rd grade reading comprehension practice using the poem Dog Language

items.question_content:
Read the poem. 

##Dog Language## 
 
by Marion Hovey Brigg

![A happy dog wagging its tail and licking its owner's face.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9017cc9fca3862e62a158ba9b970a183.jpg)

1. Our Towser is the finest dog that ever wore a collar,    
We wouldn't sell him—no, indeed—not even for a dollar!  
I understand his language now, 'cause honest, it appears  
That dogs can talk, and say a lot, with just their tails and ears.  

2. When I come home from school he meets me with a joyous bound,  
And shakes that long tail sideways, down and up, and round and round.  
Pa says he's going to hang a rug beside the door to see  
If Towser will not [[☃ definition 1]] it while he's busy greeting me.  

3. Then when he sees me get my hat, but thinks he cannot go,  
His ears get limp, his tail drops down, and he just walks off—slow;  
Though if I say the magic words: "Well, Towser, want to come?"  
Why, say! You'd know he answered "Yes," although at speech he's [[☃ definition 2]].  

[[☃ explanation 1]]

**What evidence from stanzas 1 and 2  best shows that the speaker cares for Towser?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

An old-fashioned term that meant "unable to speak". We no longer use the word *dumb* this way.

![A happy dog wagging its tail and licking its owner's face.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9017cc9fca3862e62a158ba9b970a183.jpg)

1. Our Towser is the finest dog that ever wore a collar,    
We wouldn't sell him—no, indeed—not even for a dollar!  
I understand his language now, 'cause honest, it appears  
That dogs can talk, and say a lot, with just their tails and ears.  

2. When I come home from school he meets me with a joyous bound,  
And shakes that long tail sideways, down and up, and round and round.  
Pa says he's going to hang a rug beside the door to see  
If Towser will not [[☃ definition 3]] it while he's busy greeting me.  

3. Then when he sees me get my hat, but thinks he cannot go,  
His ears get limp, his tail drops down, and he just walks off—slow;  
Though if I say the magic words: "Well, Towser, want to come?"  
Why, say! You'd know he answered "Yes," although at speech he's [[☃ definition 4]].  

[[☃ explanation 2]]

**How does Towser feel about his owner?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which line from the poem tells us how Towser feels about his owner?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

items.question_content:
Read the story, then answer the practice question.

___

##Milo and the Moon##



1. Late one night, Milo the Mouse sat on his windowsill. He stared at the moon. Its surface was full of craters. “That looks like cheese,” said Milo, who couldn’t see the moon very clearly. His stomach rumbled. He was awfully hungry! “I’m going to go to the moon and get some cheese,” he declared.

2. “Go to sleep, Milo,” said his mother.

![Milo dreaming he is riding on a bird's back to the moon.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/978823cb38c04ff2204b6151672b97d1.jpg)

3. So Milo went to sleep, and as he slept, he dreamed. Milo dreamed of flying to the moon on the back of a bird. Up and up they soared, until they landed on the moon. The moon was soft in parts, like cream cheese, and firm in parts, like cheddar, and even bouncy in parts, like mozzarella (and that part of the moon was Milo’s favorite). He ate and ate all night.

4. When Milo woke the next morning, he realized he had been chewing on his pillow, and not cheese. But he remembered his dream. He decided to go find a bird.

5. Milo left the house, and ran into his friend Tiny, the squirrel. “Where ya going?” Tiny asked.

6. “The moon!” Milo replied. “The moon is made of cheese, and I’m hungry.”

7. Tiny laughed. “The moon isn’t made of cheese! It’s rocky and rough. It only looks smooth from here.”

8. “We’ll see about that, Tiny! The next time you see me, I promise I’ll have cheese,” said Milo.

9. Milo climbed the tallest hill in the forest and waved until a bird landed in front of him. “Hello!” said Milo happily. “Would you take me to the moon, please?”

10. The bird, who was an owl, looked at the hill. Then she craned her head up high to look at the sky. “I think that’s further than I can fly, Milo!” she said. “But I do have a way to see the moon up close, without flying very far at all. It’s called a telescope.”

11. When Milo looked through the telescope, he got a really good look at the moon. “Oh!” said Milo. “It really isn’t made of cheese after all.”

12. “Nope,” said the owl. “It’s made of rocks.”

13. “Well, where am I going to get some cheese?” Milo cried as he realized his misunderstanding.

14. The owl moved the telescope with her wing, and Milo continued to look. “Oh my goodness,” he said. “A cheese store!”

15. The owl peeked through the telescope. “It looks like they’re having a sale on cheddar,” she said.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

___

##Practice question##

Read the sentence, and select the correct word from the dropdown menu.

**The author uses the word [[☃ dropdown 1]] to show that Milo thinks about his dream after he wakes up.**

choices.content: remembered
morning
flying
decided

11. When Milo looked through the telescope, he got a really good look at the moon. “Oh!” said Milo. “It really isn’t made of cheese after all.”

12. “Nope,” said the owl. “It’s made of rocks.”

13. “Well, where am I going to get some cheese?” Milo cried as he realized his misunderstanding.

14. The owl moved the telescope with her wing, and Milo continued to look. “Oh my goodness,” he said. “A cheese store!”

15. The owl peeked through the telescope. “It looks like they’re having a sale on cheddar,” she said.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

##Little Red Riding Hood##

items.question_content:

___

##Little Red Riding Hood##

1. Once there was a little girl with a cloak that had a red velvet hood. She was known as Little Red Riding Hood. One day her mother said, “Your granny is sick. Please take her some cakes; they will do her good. Remember to stay on the path, and do not stop along the way.” 

2. “Yes, mother,” said Little Red Riding Hood. Her granny lived in a house in the woods. When she entered the woods, she saw a wolf. The Wolf wished to eat Little Red Riding Hood for lunch. But she did not know that, so she was not afraid of him. 

![Little Red Riding Hood on the path through the woods talking to the Wolf.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e7664764168a5658ba0c68aa5d6c5512.png)
*Picture 1*

3. “And where are you going?” he asked.

4. “To my granny’s house,” said Little Red Riding Hood.

5. “And where does your granny live?” asked the Wolf.

6. “In the woods, a short distance from here, in a cottage under three big oak trees,” said Little Red Riding Hood. 

7. _What a tasty snack this girl would be,_ the Wolf thought, _but she’s not big enough for a meal. I must find a way to eat her and her granny too._

8. The Wolf said, “Why, look at all the pretty flowers. Why don’t you stop to rest and pick some of them?”

9. Little Red Riding Hood saw the lovely flowers, and said, “Granny would love some fresh flowers.” Forgetting what she promised her mother, she left the path and went into the woods to pick flowers. 

10. Each time she picked one, she saw others even prettier, and so she strayed deeper and deeper into the woods. 

11. The Wolf hurried to Granny’s cottage and knocked on the door.

12. “Who’s there?” said a little voice.

13. “It is I, Little Red Riding Hood,” said the Wolf.

14. “Oh, let yourself in, dear,” said Granny. 

15. The Wolf opened the door. Before Granny could realize what was happening, the Wolf gobbled her up!

16. Then the sly Wolf dressed in her nightgown. With a wicked grin, he got into the bed, and pulled up the covers. It was quite the disguise.

![The Wolf standing beside Granny's bed with a full belly, putting on her sleep bonnet.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8c937eb147fadf0a56d705cc4a5e7772.png)
*Picture 2*

17. Meanwhile, Little Red Riding Hood entered Granny’s house. She took a deep breath and called out, “Good morning, Granny.” But there was no answer. She went up to the bed. 

18. There she saw her granny, or so she thought. Little Red Riding Hood thought her granny looked very strange indeed.

19. “Oh, Granny,” she said, “what big ears you have!”

![Little Red Riding Hood looking at the Wolf tucked into bed, disguised as Granny.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8270556d572abd6674ca978d44411bed.png)
*Picture 3*


20. “The better to hear you with,” said the Wolf.

21. "Hmmm,” said Little Red Riding Hood. “Granny must be very sick, for her voice is much deeper than it used to be.”

22. “And Granny, what big eyes you have!” said Little Red Riding Hood.

23. “The better to see you with, my dear,” answered the Wolf. 

24. “And Granny, what big teeth you have!” said Little Red Riding Hood.

25. “The better to eat you with!” cried the Wolf as he sprang up and swallowed Little Red Riding Hood in one big gulp.

26. After his meal, the Wolf went to sleep, and began to snore. A hunter who was passing by the cottage heard the snoring. 

27. “My,” he said, “the old woman sounds terrible! I’d better check on her.”

28. The hunter saw the Wolf. He realized that the Wolf had eaten the old woman. He knew he had to set her free.

29. The hunter set them free, and out jumped Little Red Riding Hood and Granny.

##Little Red Riding Hood##

1. Once there was a little girl with a cloak that had a red velvet hood. She was known as Little Red Riding Hood. One day her mother said, “Your granny is sick. Please take her some cakes; they will do her good. Remember to stay on the path, and do not stop along the way.” 

2. “Yes, mother,” said Little Red Riding Hood. Her granny lived in a house in the woods. When she entered the woods, she saw a wolf. The Wolf wished to eat Little Red Riding Hood for lunch. But she did not know that, so she was not afraid of him. 

![Little Red Riding Hood on the path through the woods talking to the Wolf.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e7664764168a5658ba0c68aa5d6c5512.png)
*Picture 1*

3. “And where are you going?” he asked.

4. “To my granny’s house,” said Little Red Riding Hood.

5. “And where does your granny live?” asked the Wolf.

6. “In the woods, a short distance from here, in a cottage under three big oak trees,” said Little Red Riding Hood. 

7. _What a tasty snack this girl would be,_ the Wolf thought, _but she’s not big enough for a meal. I must find a way to eat her and her granny too._

8. The Wolf said, “Why, look at all the pretty flowers. Why don’t you stop to rest and pick some of them?”

9. Little Red Riding Hood saw the lovely flowers, and said, “Granny would love some fresh flowers.” Forgetting what she promised her mother, she left the path and went into the woods to pick flowers. 

10. Each time she picked one, she saw others even prettier, and so she strayed deeper and deeper into the woods. 

11. The Wolf hurried to Granny’s cottage and knocked on the door.

12. “Who’s there?” said a little voice.

13. “It is I, Little Red Riding Hood,” said the Wolf.

14. “Oh, let yourself in, dear,” said Granny. 

15. The Wolf opened the door. Before Granny could realize what was happening, the Wolf gobbled her up!

16. Then the sly Wolf dressed in her nightgown. With a wicked grin, he got into the bed, and pulled up the covers. It was quite the disguise.

![The Wolf standing beside Granny's bed with a full belly, putting on her sleep bonnet.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8c937eb147fadf0a56d705cc4a5e7772.png)
*Picture 2*

17. Meanwhile, Little Red Riding Hood entered Granny’s house. She took a deep breath and called out, “Good morning, Granny.” But there was no answer. She went up to the bed. 

18. There she saw her granny, or so she thought. Little Red Riding Hood thought her granny looked very strange indeed.

19. “Oh, Granny,” she said, “what big ears you have!”

![Little Red Riding Hood looking at the Wolf tucked into bed, disguised as Granny.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8270556d572abd6674ca978d44411bed.png)
*Picture 3*


20. “The better to hear you with,” said the Wolf.

21. "Hmmm,” said Little Red Riding Hood. “Granny must be very sick, for her voice is much deeper than it used to be.”

22. “And Granny, what big eyes you have!” said Little Red Riding Hood.

23. “The better to see you with, my dear,” answered the Wolf. 

24. “And Granny, what big teeth you have!” said Little Red Riding Hood.

25. “The better to eat you with!” cried the Wolf as he sprang up and swallowed Little Red Riding Hood in one big gulp.

26. After his meal, the Wolf went to sleep, and began to snore. A hunter who was passing by the cottage heard the snoring. 

27. “My,” he said, “the old woman sounds terrible! I’d better check on her.”

28. The hunter saw the Wolf. He realized that the Wolf had eaten the old woman. He knew he had to set her free.

29. The hunter set them free, and out jumped Little Red Riding Hood and Granny.

![The hunter helping Little Red Riding Hood and Granny climb out of the Wolf's belly.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/22ff473b76f8f0aa470a5c18ffcbd07a.png)
*Picture 4*

30. “Oh, I’m so grateful you saved us!” said Little Red Riding Hood.

31. When the Wolf woke up, he ran away, never to be seen again.

32. And Little Red Riding Hood said to herself, “After this I shall always do as my mother tells me.”

16. Then the sly Wolf dressed in her nightgown. With a wicked grin, he got into the bed, and pulled up the covers. It was quite the disguise.

![The Wolf standing beside Granny's bed with a full belly, putting on her sleep bonnet.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8c937eb147fadf0a56d705cc4a5e7772.png)
*Picture 2*

17. Meanwhile, Little Red Riding Hood entered Granny’s house. She took a deep breath and called out, “Good morning, Granny.” But there was no answer. She went up to the bed. 

18. There she saw her granny, or so she thought. Little Red Riding Hood thought her granny looked very strange indeed.

19. “Oh, Granny,” she said, “what big ears you have!”

![Little Red Riding Hood looking at the Wolf tucked into bed, disguised as Granny.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8270556d572abd6674ca978d44411bed.png)
*Picture 3*


20. “The better to hear you with,” said the Wolf.

21. "Hmmm,” said Little Red Riding Hood. “Granny must be very sick, for her voice is much deeper than it used to be.”

22. “And Granny, what big eyes you have!” said Little Red Riding Hood.

23. “The better to see you with, my dear,” answered the Wolf. 

24. “And Granny, what big teeth you have!” said Little Red Riding Hood.

25. “The better to eat you with!” cried the Wolf as he sprang up and swallowed Little Red Riding Hood in one big gulp.

26. After his meal, the Wolf went to sleep, and began to snore. A hunter who was passing by the cottage heard the snoring. 

27. “My,” he said, “the old woman sounds terrible! I’d better check on her.”

28. The hunter saw the Wolf. He realized that the Wolf had eaten the old woman. He knew he had to set her free.

29. The hunter set them free, and out jumped Little Red Riding Hood and Granny.

![The hunter helping Little Red Riding Hood and Granny climb out of the Wolf's belly.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/22ff473b76f8f0aa470a5c18ffcbd07a.png)
*Picture 4*

30. “Oh, I’m so grateful you saved us!” said Little Red Riding Hood.

31. When the Wolf woke up, he ran away, never to be seen again.

32. And Little Red Riding Hood said to herself, “After this I shall always do as my mother tells me.”

##The Little Yellow House##

![An illustration of the Little Yellow House by a forest, with a duck pond in front.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9b4971d04e06377f486f521b2dbdd78f.jpg)
*Picture 1*

1. The Little Yellow House stood in a meadow surrounded by a thick stand of trees. Nearby, there were raspberry bushes and peach trees. There was a small pond with ducks that quacked happily all day long. Little Yellow House loved watching the squirrels scampering in search of food, and seeing the gopher burrowing underground. She loved watching the trees sway in the wind and drop colorful leaves in the fall. It was a lovely, peaceful location.

2. Little Yellow House also loved her family. The father and mother farmed the land. The little boy and little girl played in the yard and read books on the front porch. The family chatted around the dinner table every evening. Little Yellow House was very, very happy.

3. Years passed. Soon the children grew up and moved away. The trees of the forest slowly disappeared. Roads and homes started __popping up__ all around—but thankfully, there was not a house too close to her yet. 

4. One day a large truck came and dropped dirt into the pond. Soon the pond was nothing but a hole filled with dirt. The ducks flew away. Little Yellow House was very, very sad. 

![The Little Yellow House frowning while a dump truck does construction.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e6334330a03f905a3fc385d69f8927f3.jpg)
*Picture 2*

5. Not long after, Little Yellow House noticed a flurry of activity where the pond once stood. Soon another house appeared! Little Yellow House was not happy.

6. “How dare a house come so close to me!” she said to a squirrel. “Especially one that made my friends, the ducks, leave.” 

7. One morning Little Yellow House heard something that she never heard before. “Yoo hoo! Little Yellow House! My name is Little Blue House, and I am new to the neighborhood.”

8. Little Yellow House didn’t say anything. She was still mad. She didn’t like all this change. 

9. Soon more things began to change. A park was built with a small pond. The ducks came back! This made the Little Yellow House happier.

10. Then, more houses started to pop up. Children filled the streets and yards. They were playing games and having fun. Little Yellow House enjoyed watching the children laughing and smiling. Soon, she began to think to herself, “Maybe these new houses aren’t so bad. If there weren’t any new houses the children wouldn’t be here. I love to watch the children play.”

11. One spring morning, her new neighbors, Little Brown House and Little Green House, said, “Yoo hoo! Little Yellow House, don’t you just love to watch the children play? Don’t they look like they are having so much fun?”

12. Then it occurred to Little Yellow House that the other houses were trying to be friends. All she had ever known was the meadow, the forest, the animals, and the family. She remembered how the family talked every evening around the dinner table. She wanted someone to talk with about her day, too.

![The three houses smiling together while kids play in front.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9173d8db1beeff06223464356275d3ed.jpg)
*Picture 3*

13. Little Yellow House felt her heart open up. She replied, “Yes. I do love watching the children play. I also like to watch the ducks splash in the pond.”

14. “Me too!” said Little Brown House.

15. “The ducks are so much fun to watch!” said Little Green House. 

16. Little Yellow House smiled to herself. She was glad that she took a chance and spoke to Little Green House and Little Brown House. Now she had others to share her feelings with.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

___

11. One spring morning, her new neighbors, Little Brown House and Little Green House, said, “Yoo hoo! Little Yellow House, don’t you just love to watch the children play? Don’t they look like they are having so much fun?”

12. Then it occurred to Little Yellow House that the other houses were trying to be friends. All she had ever known was the meadow, the forest, the animals, and the family. She remembered how the family talked every evening around the dinner table. She wanted someone to talk with about her day, too.

![The three houses smiling together while kids play in front.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9173d8db1beeff06223464356275d3ed.jpg)
*Picture 3*

13. Little Yellow House felt her heart open up. She replied, “Yes. I do love watching the children play. I also like to watch the ducks splash in the pond.”

14. “Me too!” said Little Brown House.

15. “The ducks are so much fun to watch!” said Little Green House. 

16. Little Yellow House smiled to herself. She was glad that she took a chance and spoke to Little Green House and Little Brown House. Now she had others to share her feelings with.

##Tom Thumb##

![Baby Tom Thumb in a tea cup, with his parents smiling down at him.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e2afa6f84be0c36fcde51703173f5f9d.png)
*Picture 1*

**Cast of Characters:**   
**Tom Thumb:** a very small boy, no bigger than an adult’s thumb  
**Randy:** a robber  
**Jed:** another robber

**Scene One:**  
(_Evening, in a barn outside the mayor’s house. TOM THUMB has just run away from a man that he was traveling with._)  
**Tom:** _(talking to AUDIENCE)_ Phew, what a day of adventure, eh friends? It was easy to escape from that silly man—because I’m so small. All I had to do was jump into a mousehole, and he couldn’t find me anywhere. I may be tiny, but I can still be tricky!
(_Sound of footsteps._)   
**Tom:** Someone’s coming—let’s hide!   
(_TOM hides. Enter TWO ROBBERS, JED and RANDY._)   
**Randy:** This is the house, Jed. The mayor’s traveled a long distance and won’t be back until tomorrow, so tonight is the perfect night to steal all his money.   
**Jed:**  But what about the cook and the maid? They’re certain to hear us breaking those famous iron bars around his money box.   
**Randy:** Then we’ll just have to do it very, very quietly, won’t we?   
**Tom:** (_to AUDIENCE_) Robbers! I like to play tricks, but stealing isn’t right. I feel another adventure coming on . . . watch this.   
(_TOM jumps out._)   
**Tom:** (_to ROBBERS_) Hello there!   
**Randy:** (_looking around_) Who said that?   
**Tom:** (_waving_) Down here!    
**Jed:** How curious. What a tiny fellow.

![Tom Thumb waving hello to Jed and Randy.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/aacbcbd9eaabf13a692e75a2c0200ee1.png)
*Picture 2*

**Tom:** Please take me with you into the house—I would love to help you.    
**Randy:** You? Ha! You’re very polite, but you’re rather small to be a robber.   
**Tom:** You said the mayor’s money is behind iron bars, and it will be noisy if you break them. But I’m so small, I can slip right between the bars without a sound.   
**Jed:** And why should we trust you?   
**Tom:** I may be tiny, but I’m a faithful fellow—and I love adventures!  
**Randy:** Alright then, tiny one. Just remember—everyone has to speak very quietly inside the house. Think you can do that?  
**Tom:** Oh, certainly!  
**Randy:** Very well. Now, let’s get robbing.

**Scene Two:**    
(_Inside the mayor’s house._)   
**Randy:** (_whispering_) Now then, tiny one. I expect climbing that table will be easy for you—then you just have to squeeze through the iron bars to get us the money. Ready?   
**Tom:** (_whispering_) Ready!   
**Randy:** (_whispering_) Remember: quiet as a mouse!   
(_TOM climbs up the table, and slips through the iron bars._)   
**Tom:** (_to AUDIENCE, whispering_) Now watch this! (_To ROBBERS, talking in his loudest voice_) HOW MUCH DO YOU WANT? SHOULD I JUST TAKE ALL OF IT?   
**Randy:** (_whispering_) SSSShhhhh! You’ll wake the cook and the maid—just give us the money!  
**Tom:** (_Even louder_) WHAT? YOU WANT ME TO TAKE ALL OF THE SEVERAL HUNDRED COINS OUT OF THIS MONEY BOX?   
(_Sound of a door banging upstairs, and people shouting and running._)   
**Jed:** (_whispering_) They’re coming, Randy!

![The robbers trying to hush Tom Thumb, as the maid comes down the stairs to investigate the noise.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8e1b492eb11abf800c8219488c70d3ff.png)
*Picture 3*

**Randy:** (_whispering_) You sneaky little rat-boy—you tricked us! Let’s get out of here, Jed.   
(_ROBBERS run away._)   
**Tom:** (_shouting after ROBBERS_) I may be tiny, but I can still be tricky!

[[☃ explanation 3]]

___

##Practice Question##

**What happens at the end of the story that makes the robbers run away?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

This does not in any way imply that the Core Knowledge® Foundation endorses this work._

The robbers talk too loudly to each other.

**Tom:** (_waving_) Down here!    
**Jed:** How curious. What a tiny fellow.

**Scene One:**  
(_Evening, in a barn outside the mayor’s house. TOM THUMB has just run away from a man that he was traveling with._)  
**Tom:** _(talking to AUDIENCE)_ Phew, what a day of adventure, eh friends? It was easy to escape from that silly man—because I’m so small. All I had to do was jump into a mousehole, and he couldn’t find me anywhere. I may be tiny, but I can still be tricky!
(_Sound of footsteps._)   
**Tom:** Someone’s coming—let’s hide!   
(_TOM hides. Enter TWO ROBBERS, JED and RANDY._)   
**Randy:** This is the house, Jed. The mayor’s traveled a long distance and won’t be back until tomorrow, so tonight is the perfect night to steal all his money.   
**Jed:**  But what about the cook and the maid? They’re certain to hear us breaking those famous iron bars around his money box.   
**Randy:** Then we’ll just have to do it very, very quietly, won’t we?   
**Tom:** (_to AUDIENCE_) Robbers! I like to play tricks, but stealing isn’t right. I feel another adventure coming on . . . watch this.   
(_TOM jumps out._)   
**Tom:** (_to ROBBERS_) Hello there!   
**Randy:** (_looking around_) Who said that?   
**Tom:** (_waving_) Down here!    
**Jed:** How curious. What a tiny fellow.

![Tom Thumb waving hello to Jed and Randy.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/aacbcbd9eaabf13a692e75a2c0200ee1.png)
*Picture 2*

**Tom:** Please take me with you into the house—I would love to help you.    
**Randy:** You? Ha! You’re very polite, but you’re rather small to be a robber.   
**Tom:** You said the mayor’s money is behind iron bars, and it will be noisy if you break them. But I’m so small, I can slip right between the bars without a sound.   
**Jed:** And why should we trust you?   
**Tom:** I may be tiny, but I’m a faithful fellow—and I love adventures!  
**Randy:** Alright then, tiny one. Just remember—everyone has to speak very quietly inside the house. Think you can do that?  
**Tom:** Oh, certainly!  
**Randy:** Very well. Now, let’s get robbing.

**Scene Two:**    
(_Inside the mayor’s house._)   
**Randy:** (_whispering_) Now then, tiny one. I expect climbing that table will be easy for you—then you just have to squeeze through the iron bars to get us the money. Ready?   
**Tom:** (_whispering_) Ready!   
**Randy:** (_whispering_) Remember: quiet as a mouse!   
(_TOM climbs up the table, and slips through the iron bars._)   
**Tom:** (_to AUDIENCE, whispering_) Now watch this! (_To ROBBERS, talking in his loudest voice_) HOW MUCH DO YOU WANT? SHOULD I JUST TAKE ALL OF IT?   
**Randy:** (_whispering_) SSSShhhhh! You’ll wake the cook and the maid—just give us the money!  
**Tom:** (_Even louder_) WHAT? YOU WANT ME TO TAKE ALL OF THE SEVERAL HUNDRED COINS OUT OF THIS MONEY BOX?   
(_Sound of a door banging upstairs, and people shouting and running._)   
**Jed:** (_whispering_) They’re coming, Randy!

![The robbers trying to hush Tom Thumb, as the maid comes down the stairs to investigate the noise.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8e1b492eb11abf800c8219488c70d3ff.png)
*Picture 3*

**Randy:** (_whispering_) You sneaky little rat-boy—you tricked us! Let’s get out of here, Jed.   
(_ROBBERS run away._)   
**Tom:** (_shouting after ROBBERS_) I may be tiny, but I can still be tricky!

##A Tiny Girl: Thumbelina## 

Once upon a time  
a tiny girl grew  
from out of a flower sparkling with dew.

![Thumbelina sitting in a red tulip.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f7ab7dcff50dc4fc79c987783a4b03ea.png)
*Picture 1*

She was kind, __gentle__,   
and lived without strife  
‘til a toad stole her as she slept in the night.  

The toad trapped the girl.  
She could not __escape__.   
How mean! How __wicked__! What a horrible fate! 

“Help me!” the girl cried.  
A fish heard her plea.   
He nibbled and nibbled to set the girl free. 

![Thumbelina kneeling on a lily pad, smiling down at the goldfish.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/573c30e45f46d1e23d60cbb859110f68.png)
*Picture 2*

Soon it was winter.   
The girl met a mouse.   
“Come stay with me in my warm winter house.” 

She sang for the mouse  
and all of her guests—  
including a mole who liked the girl best.   

“You will be my wife,”  
he ordered one night.     
“Oh no!” gasped the girl, shivering in fright.  

The girl did not wish  
a life underground.   
She fled on a bird. “I hope I’m not found!” 

But when she landed—  
Oh! What a surprise!    
A tiny young king was just the same size! 

![Thumbelina greeting the tiny king on his flower thrown.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/23eefda929bdb46790defb9d478cd353.png)
*Picture 3*

He ruled the land   
of those small and fair.   
And so Thumbelina lived happily there. 

[[☃ explanation 1]]

___

##Practice Question##

**What information from the beginning of the poem helps you understand the rest of the poem?**

Understanding that Thumbelina misses her family helps explain why she won’t sing.

![Thumbelina sitting in a red tulip.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f7ab7dcff50dc4fc79c987783a4b03ea.png)
*Picture 1*

She was kind, __gentle__,   
and lived without strife  
‘til a toad stole her as she slept in the night.  

The toad trapped the girl.  
She could not __escape__.   
How mean! How __wicked__! What a horrible fate! 

“Help me!” the girl cried.  
A fish heard her plea.   
He nibbled and nibbled to set the girl free. 

![Thumbelina kneeling on a lily pad, smiling down at the goldfish.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/573c30e45f46d1e23d60cbb859110f68.png)
*Picture 2*

Soon it was winter.   
The girl met a mouse.   
“Come stay with me in my warm winter house.” 

She sang for the mouse  
and all of her guests—  
including a mole who liked the girl best.   

“You will be my wife,”  
he ordered one night.     
“Oh no!” gasped the girl, shivering in fright.  

The girl did not wish  
a life underground.   
She fled on a bird. “I hope I’m not found!” 

But when she landed—  
Oh! What a surprise!    
A tiny young king was just the same size! 

![Thumbelina greeting the tiny king on his flower thrown.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/23eefda929bdb46790defb9d478cd353.png)
*Picture 3*

He ruled the land   
of those small and fair.   
And so Thumbelina lived happily there. 

[[☃ explanation 1]]

##Tom Thumb##

![Baby Tom Thumb in a tea cup, with his parents smiling down at him.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e2afa6f84be0c36fcde51703173f5f9d.png)
*Picture 1*

**Cast of Characters:**   
**Tom Thumb:** a very small boy, no bigger than an adult’s thumb  
**Randy:** a robber  
**Jed:** another robber

**Scene One:**  
(_Evening, in a barn outside the mayor’s house. TOM THUMB has just run away from a man that he was traveling with._)  
**Tom:** _(talking to AUDIENCE)_ Phew, what a day of adventure, eh friends? It was easy to escape from that silly man—because I’m so small. All I had to do was jump into a mousehole, and he couldn’t find me anywhere. I may be tiny, but I can still be tricky!
(_Sound of footsteps._)   
**Tom:** Someone’s coming—let’s hide!   
(_TOM hides. Enter TWO ROBBERS, JED and RANDY._)   
**Randy:** This is the house, Jed. The mayor’s traveled a long distance and won’t be back until tomorrow, so tonight is the perfect night to steal all his money.   
**Jed:**  But what about the cook and the maid? They’re certain to hear us breaking those famous iron bars around his money box.   
**Randy:** Then we’ll just have to do it very, very quietly, won’t we?   
**Tom:** (_to AUDIENCE_) Robbers! I like to play tricks, but stealing isn’t right. I feel another adventure coming on . . . watch this.   
(_TOM jumps out._)   
**Tom:** (_to ROBBERS_) Hello there!   
**Randy:** (_looking around_) Who said that?   
**Tom:** (_waving_) Down here!    
**Jed:** How curious. What a tiny fellow.

![Tom Thumb waving hello to Jed and Randy.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/aacbcbd9eaabf13a692e75a2c0200ee1.png)
*Picture 2*

**Tom:** Please take me with you into the house—I would love to help you.    
**Randy:** You? Ha! You’re very polite, but you’re rather small to be a robber.   
**Tom:** You said the mayor’s money is behind iron bars, and it will be noisy if you break them. But I’m so small, I can slip right between the bars without a sound.   
**Jed:** And why should we trust you?   
**Tom:** I may be tiny, but I’m a faithful fellow—and I love adventures!  
**Randy:** Alright then, tiny one. Just remember—everyone has to speak very quietly inside the house. Think you can do that?  
**Tom:** Oh, certainly!  
**Randy:** Very well. Now, let’s get robbing.

**Scene Two:**    
(_Inside the mayor’s house._)   
**Randy:** (_whispering_) Now then, tiny one. I expect climbing that table will be easy for you—then you just have to squeeze through the iron bars to get us the money. Ready?   
**Tom:** (_whispering_) Ready!   
**Randy:** (_whispering_) Remember: quiet as a mouse!   
(_TOM climbs up the table, and slips through the iron bars._)   
**Tom:** (_to AUDIENCE, whispering_) Now watch this! (_To ROBBERS, talking in his loudest voice_) HOW MUCH DO YOU WANT? SHOULD I JUST TAKE ALL OF IT?   
**Randy:** (_whispering_) SSSShhhhh! You’ll wake the cook and the maid—just give us the money!  
**Tom:** (_Even louder_) WHAT? YOU WANT ME TO TAKE ALL OF THE SEVERAL HUNDRED COINS OUT OF THIS MONEY BOX?   
(_Sound of a door banging upstairs, and people shouting and running._)   
**Jed:** (_whispering_) They’re coming, Randy!

![The robbers trying to hush Tom Thumb, as the maid comes down the stairs to investigate the noise.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8e1b492eb11abf800c8219488c70d3ff.png)
*Picture 3*

**Randy:** (_whispering_) You sneaky little rat-boy—you tricked us! Let’s get out of here, Jed.   
(_ROBBERS run away._)   
**Tom:** (_shouting after ROBBERS_) I may be tiny, but I can still be tricky!


[[☃ explanation 4]]

___

##Practice Question##

**Pick two ways the stories “Tom Thumb” and “Thumbelina” are the same.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

items: A character plays a sneaky trick.
A group of robbers try to sneak into a home to steal money.
A small person uses smart thinking.
A toad traps a young girl.
A small girl and a small king live in a small kingdom.

items.question_content:
Read the poem, then answer the practice question.  

___

##A Tiny Girl: Thumbelina## 

Once upon a time  
a tiny girl grew  
from out of a flower sparkling with dew.

![Thumbelina sitting in a red tulip.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f7ab7dcff50dc4fc79c987783a4b03ea.png)
*Picture 1*

She was kind, __gentle__,   
and lived without strife  
‘til a toad stole her as she slept in the night.  

The toad trapped the girl.  
She could not __escape__.   
How mean! How __wicked__! What a horrible fate! 

“Help me!” the girl cried.  
A fish heard her plea.   
He nibbled and nibbled to set the girl free. 

![Thumbelina kneeling on a lily pad, smiling down at the goldfish.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/573c30e45f46d1e23d60cbb859110f68.png)
*Picture 2*

Soon it was winter.   
The girl met a mouse.   
“Come stay with me in my warm winter house.” 

She sang for the mouse  
and all of her guests—  
including a mole who liked the girl best.   

“You will be my wife,”  
he ordered one night.     
“Oh no!” gasped the girl, shivering in fright.  

The girl did not wish  
a life underground.   
She fled on a bird. “I hope I’m not found!” 

But when she landed—  
Oh! What a surprise!    
A tiny young king was just the same size! 

![Thumbelina greeting the tiny king on his flower thrown.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/23eefda929bdb46790defb9d478cd353.png)
*Picture 3*

He ruled the land   
of those small and fair.   
And so Thumbelina lived happily there. 

[[☃ explanation 1]]

___

##Practice Question##

**Drag and drop each speaker to the quote from the poem.**

[[☃ matcher 1]]

I thought there was a rabbit inside the moon,

items.question_content:

___

##The Beautiful Mysterious Moon##

![Carlos reading a book and the poem's speaker smiling while the moon shines in the background.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3bff695453cf0a28e91008ba6f95726f.png)
 
I thought there was a rabbit inside the moon,     
One that nibbled on lettuce and __mushrooms__.    
But Carlos says that those are just craters.   
 
*That’s the way the moon works, the beautiful mysterious moon.*
 
I thought there was a giant in the sea    
That made the waves and pushed them onto the beach.    
But Carlos says that those are just tides.   
 
*That’s the way the moon works, the beautiful mysterious moon.*
 
I thought there was an old lady under the underground   
With a giant magnet that held us in place when we walk around.   
But Carlos says that it’s just gravity.   

*That’s the way the moon works, the beautiful mysterious moon.*

I don’t know if I agree or disagree,   
That the moon is responsible for tides and gravity.   
But Carlos read the science book, so we’ll have to believe   
That thing in the sky has an effect on you and me.   

[[☃ explanation 1]]

___

##Practice question##

**What TWO reasons explain why the poet uses the word __mushrooms__ in the second line of the poem?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.explanation: NP: Non-Prose

choices.content: because the *oo* sound in __mushroom__ is the same sound as the *oo* in “moon”
because the top of a mushroom is round, like a moon crater
because the moon tastes like __mushrooms__
because the *ush* sound in __mushroom__ rhymes  with the *uce* sound in  “lettuce”
because there are __mushrooms__ growing on the moon

##Little Red Riding Hood##

1. Once there was a little girl with a cloak that had a red velvet hood. She was known as Little Red Riding Hood. One day her mother said, “Your granny is sick. Please take her some cakes; they will do her good. Remember to stay on the path, and do not stop along the way.” 

2. “Yes, mother,” said Little Red Riding Hood. Her granny lived in a house in the woods. When she entered the woods, she saw a wolf. The Wolf wished to eat Little Red Riding Hood for lunch. But she did not know that, so she was not afraid of him. 

![Little Red Riding Hood on the path through the woods talking to the Wolf.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e7664764168a5658ba0c68aa5d6c5512.png)
*Picture 1*

3. “And where are you going?” he asked.

4. “To my granny’s house,” said Little Red Riding Hood.

5. “And where does your granny live?” asked the Wolf.

6. “In the woods, a short distance from here, in a cottage under three big oak trees,” said Little Red Riding Hood. 

7. _What a tasty snack this girl would be,_ the Wolf thought, _but she’s not big enough for a meal. I must find a way to eat her and her granny too._

8. The Wolf said, “Why, look at all the pretty flowers. Why don’t you stop to rest and pick some of them?”

9. Little Red Riding Hood saw the lovely flowers, and said, “Granny would love some fresh flowers.” Forgetting what she promised her mother, she left the path and went into the woods to pick flowers. 

10. Each time she picked one, she saw others even prettier, and so she strayed deeper and deeper into the woods. 

11. The Wolf hurried to Granny’s cottage and knocked on the door.

12. “Who’s there?” said a little voice.

13. “It is I, Little Red Riding Hood,” said the Wolf.

14. “Oh, let yourself in, dear,” said Granny. 

15. The Wolf opened the door. Before Granny could realize what was happening, the Wolf gobbled her up!

16. Then the sly Wolf dressed in her nightgown. With a wicked grin, he got into the bed, and pulled up the covers. It was quite the disguise.

![The Wolf standing beside Granny's bed with a full belly, putting on her sleep bonnet.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8c937eb147fadf0a56d705cc4a5e7772.png)
*Picture 2*

17. Meanwhile, Little Red Riding Hood entered Granny’s house. She took a deep breath and called out, “Good morning, Granny.” But there was no answer. She went up to the bed. 

18. There she saw her granny, or so she thought. Little Red Riding Hood thought her granny looked very strange indeed.

19. “Oh, Granny,” she said, “what big ears you have!”

![Little Red Riding Hood looking at the Wolf tucked into bed, disguised as Granny.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8270556d572abd6674ca978d44411bed.png)
*Picture 3*


20. “The better to hear you with,” said the Wolf.

21. "Hmmm,” said Little Red Riding Hood. “Granny must be very sick, for her voice is much deeper than it used to be.”

22. “And Granny, what big eyes you have!” said Little Red Riding Hood.

23. “The better to see you with, my dear,” answered the Wolf. 

24. “And Granny, what big teeth you have!” said Little Red Riding Hood.

25. “The better to eat you with!” cried the Wolf as he sprang up and swallowed Little Red Riding Hood in one big gulp.

26. After his meal, the Wolf went to sleep, and began to snore. A hunter who was passing by the cottage heard the snoring. 

27. “My,” he said, “the old woman sounds terrible! I’d better check on her.”

28. The hunter saw the Wolf. He realized that the Wolf had eaten the old woman. He knew he had to set her free.

29. The hunter set them free, and out jumped Little Red Riding Hood and Granny.

![The hunter helping Little Red Riding Hood and Granny climb out of the Wolf's belly.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/22ff473b76f8f0aa470a5c18ffcbd07a.png)
*Picture 4*

30. “Oh, I’m so grateful you saved us!” said Little Red Riding Hood.

31. When the Wolf woke up, he ran away, never to be seen again.

32. And Little Red Riding Hood said to herself, “After this I shall always do as my mother tells me.” 

[[☃ explanation 1]]

---

24. “And Granny, what big teeth you have!” said Little Red Riding Hood.

28. The hunter saw the Wolf. He realized that the Wolf had eaten the old woman. He knew he had to set her free.
